title: Soft white rolls
subtitle: Butter rolls for the bread basket or the burger bun
excerpt: Twelve soft white rolls with a thin pale crust, a fluffy crumb, and a slight sweetness from a small amount of caster sugar. Mixed, shaped, proved and baked in under three hours. Good for sandwiches, burgers, or served warm at a dinner table.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the enriched white roll section documents the use of a small amount of fat and milk in the dough to produce a softer crust than a lean dough baked on a tray. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: notes the milk-enriched small roll as the natural companion to afternoon tea and dinner service in the mid-Victorian kitchen.

content.text: A soft white roll is the workhorse of the dinner table and the bread basket. This is an enriched dough: butter and milk go into the mix alongside the standard flour, water, yeast, and salt. The fat coats the gluten strands, giving a finer, more tender crumb than a lean loaf, and the milk sugars help the crust colour to a pale gold rather than a deep brown. Twelve rolls, done in under three hours.

Weigh the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} into a large bowl. Keep the yeast and salt on opposite sides of the bowl. Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} through the flour until it disappears. Combine the {{whole-milk}} and {{water}} and pour into the bowl. Mix to a soft, slightly tacky dough. This is an

and it will be softer than a plain bread dough. Knead for 8 minutes by hand (or 6 minutes with a dough hook on medium speed) until smooth and elastic. The dough should clear the sides of the bowl and feel slightly tacky but not wet.
Shape the dough into a ball, return it to the bowl, and cover with a damp cloth. Leave in a warm place for 60 minutes until doubled. In a cool kitchen allow 80 minutes.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Tip the dough onto a floured surface and divide into 12 equal pieces (about 75 g each). To shape a roll, flatten a piece with your palm, fold the edges into the centre, turn seam-side down, then cup your hand over the dough and roll it against the surface in a tight circular motion. The surface tension builds and the roll tightens into a neat ball. Repeat with the remaining pieces and arrange on the prepared tray, leaving a 3 cm gap between each. They will touch when proved.
Cover the tray loosely with oiled clingfilm and leave for 40 minutes until the rolls are puffy and beginning to touch. Preheat the oven to 200°C fan while they prove.

Brush the rolls gently with the beaten {{eggs}} egg. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes until pale gold on top, with a slightly deeper colour where they have joined. Tap the base of one roll. It should sound hollow. Transfer to a rack. Eat warm if possible, or cool fully and store in a bag for up to two days.

For a seeded topping, brush with egg and scatter sesame seeds or poppy seeds before baking. For a slightly richer roll closer to a milk bread, replace the water entirely with milk, the crumb will be more cake-like and the shelf life will extend to three days.
